findings.title: Self-referential change-of-variables formula in Example 2 (missing tilde)
findings.snippet: Performing the change of variables $b_{i}=\left[\tau-b_{i}\right] / 2$ and $\beta=-\tilde{\beta} / 2$ (with the status quo equal to $\hat{b}_{i}=\left[\tau-\tilde{b}_{i}\right] / 2$)
findings.explanation: The change-of-variables formula for $b_i$ uses $b_i$ on both sides: $b_i = [\tau - b_i]/2$. This is circular — the new variable is defined in terms of itself — and is almost certainly a dropped tilde. The companion formula for the status quo correctly writes $\hat{b}_i = [\tau - \tilde{b}_i]/2$, using the original local-public-goods variable $\tilde{b}_i$. The formula for $b_i$ should be $b_i = [\tau - \tilde{b}_i]/2$ for consistency and mathematical coherence.
findings.fix: Replace $b_{i}=\left[\tau-b_{i}\right] / 2$ with $b_{i}=\left[\tau-\tilde{b}_{i}\right] / 2$ to match the status-quo formula and eliminate the self-referential definition.

findings.title: Dual terminology for the same externality concept: 'non-strategic' vs. 'pure'
findings.snippet: Introduction: "spillover effects on others' incentives, as well as non-strategic externalities" — Section 2: "$P_{i}(\boldsymbol{a}_{-i}, \boldsymbol{G}, \boldsymbol{b})$ captures pure externalities—that is, spillovers that do not affect best responses"
findings.explanation: The Introduction introduces the concept using the phrase 'non-strategic externalities,' but Section 2 names the same object 'pure externalities' (embodied in the $P_i$ term) without cross-referencing or equating the two phrases. A reader encountering Section 2 directly may not immediately recognise that 'pure externalities' is the formal counterpart of the informal 'non-strategic externalities' used in the Introduction. The inconsistency is harmless in isolation but creates a small reading friction at exactly the interface between the two sections.
findings.fix: Either (a) adopt one term consistently ('pure externalities' is already the formal term used in Section 2, so prefer it in the Introduction as well), or (b) add a brief parenthetical in the Introduction, e.g., 'non-strategic (or pure) externalities,' to signal the equivalence.

findings.title: Road-map describes Section 2 only as 'the optimal intervention problem,' omitting model setup
findings.snippet: "The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 presents the optimal intervention problem."
findings.explanation: Section 2 is titled 'THE MODEL' and devotes the bulk of its content to establishing the game (payoff function, equilibrium characterisation, Assumptions 1–2) before introducing the IT problem (equation 4). The road-map entry 'Section 2 presents the optimal intervention problem' is therefore misleading: it skips the foundational model content and leads a reader to expect that the game primitives were already fully formalised in the Introduction. In reality, the first formal definitions of $\mathcal{N}$, $U_i$, $\boldsymbol{G}$, $\beta$, and equilibrium only appear in Section 2. This creates a minor orientation mismatch at the section boundary.
findings.fix: Revise the road-map sentence to reflect both purposes, e.g., 'Section 2 sets out the model and the optimal intervention (IT) problem.' This aligns with the section title and its actual structure.
